standard_description:
Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem. For example, if each person at a party will eat 3/8 of a pound of roast beef, and there will be 5 people at the party, how many pounds of roast beef will be needed? Between what two whole numbers does your answer lie?
Grade 4 expectations in this domain are limited to fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.

items.question_content:
A zookeeper records how many scoops of peanuts she feeds the elephants for several days.


Day | Scoops
:- | :-:
Monday| $20\dfrac{7}{8}$
Tuesday| $21$
Wednesday| $19\dfrac58$

**How many more scoops of peanuts did the zookeeper feed the elephants on Tuesday than on Wednesday?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] scoops
hints: We need to subtract to find out how many more scoops of peanuts the zookeeper fed the elephants on Tuesday than on Wednesday.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then look at the fractions first:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{21} &\blueD\\\\
-&\maroonD{19}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\end{align}$

We can't subtract the $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$, because we don't have any eighths to subtract from.

$\blueD{21}$ can be regrouped so that it also has a fractional part and we can subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{21} &= \blueD{20+  1} \\
\\
&= \blueD{20 +  \dfrac{8}{8}} \\
\\
&= \blueD{20 \dfrac{8}{8}} 
\end{align}$

Now subtract the fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{20} &\blueD{\dfrac88}\\\\
-&\maroonD{19}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{3}{8}}
\end{align}$

Next, subtract the whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{20} &\blueD{\dfrac88}\\\\
-&\maroonD{19}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&
\greenD1&\greenD{\dfrac{3}{8}}
\end{align}$
The zookeeper fed the elephants $1\dfrac38$ more scoops of peanuts on Tuesday than on Wednesday.

items.question_content:
After a rain storm, Lily measures the depth of several puddles in her backyard. She records her results in a table.


Puddle |Depth (inches)
:- | :-:
On the lawn| $\dfrac34$
On the sidewalk | $\dfrac24$
Under the swing | $1\dfrac14$

**How much deeper was the puddle under the swing than the puddle on the sidewalk?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] inches
hints: We need to subtract to find out how much deeper the puddle under the swing was.

We can line up the numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then look at the fractions first. 

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{1} &\blueD{\dfrac{1}{4}}\\\\
-&&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\
\hline{}
\end{align}$

We can't subtract $\blueD{\dfrac{1}{4}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$, because $\blueD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$.

$\blueD{1\dfrac{1}{4}}$ can be regrouped so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{1\dfrac{1}{4}} &= \blueD{1+  \dfrac{1}{4}} \\
\\
&= \blueD{ \dfrac{4}{4} +  \dfrac{1}{4}} \\
\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac{5}{4}} \end{align}$

Now, subtract the fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{} &\blueD{\dfrac{5}{4}}\\\\
-&\pink{}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{3}{4}}
\end{align}$
The puddle under the swing was $\dfrac34$ inch deeper than the puddle on the sidewalk.

items.question_content:
Maya measures the weights of several fruits at the grocery store. She records her results in a table.

The watermelon weighs $7\dfrac34$ pounds more than the pineapple.

**Record the weight of the watermelon in the table.**

Fruit |Weight (pounds)
- | :-: 
Watermelon| [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
Pineapple| $1\dfrac2{4}$
Bag of apples| $5$
Banana|$\dfrac14$
hints: We need to add to find the weight of the watermelon.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then add the fractions first:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{7} &\blueD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{5}{4}}
\end{align}$

Because $\greenD{\dfrac{5}{4}}$ is greater than $1$, it must be converted into a mixed number.

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\dfrac{5}{4}} &= \greenD{\dfrac{4}{4} +  \dfrac{1}{4}} \\
\\
&= \greenD{1  \dfrac{1}{4}} 
\end{align}$

Then, we put the $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ under the fractions and the $\greenD{1}$ with the other whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\greenD{1} \\
&\blueD{7} &\blueD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{1}{4}}
\end{align}$

Next, add the whole numbers:

$\begin{align}
&\greenD{1} \\
&\blueD{7} &\blueD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{9}&\greenD{\dfrac{1}{4}}
\end{align}$
The watermelon weighed $9\dfrac14$ pounds.

items.question_content:
Alicia hikes to see a waterfall. She records the weight of the items she put in her backpack in a table. 

Alicia's camera weighs $1\dfrac35\text{ kg}$ less than her lunch.

**Record the weight of Alicia's camera in the table.**

Item|Weight (kg)
:- | :-:
Water bottle| $1\dfrac2{5}$
Camera| [[☃ numeric-input 4]]
Lunch| $2\dfrac1{5}$
hints: Alicia's camera weighs less than her lunch. So, we need to subtract to find the weight of the camera.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then look at the fractions first:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{2} &\blueD{\dfrac{1}{5}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}\\
\hline{}
\end{align}$

We can't subtract $\blueD{\dfrac{1}{5}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}$, because $\blueD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}$.

$\blueD{2\dfrac{1}{5}}$ can be regrouped so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}$.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{2\dfrac{1}{5}} &= \blueD{2+  \dfrac{1}{5}} \\
\\
&= \blueD{1 +  1 +  \dfrac{1}{5}} \\
\\
&= \blueD{1 +  \dfrac{5}{5} +  \dfrac{1}{5}} \\
\\
&= \blueD{1 \dfrac{6}{5}} 
\end{align}$

Now, subtract the fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{1} &\blueD{\dfrac{6}{5}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{}&\greenD{\dfrac{3}{5}}
\end{align}$

Next, subtract the whole numbers:


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{1} &\blueD{\dfrac{6}{5}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{0}&\greenD{\dfrac{3}{5}}
\end{align}$
Alicia's camera weighs $\dfrac35\text{ kg}$.

items.question_content:
Mia starts with a piece of paper that is $6\text{ cm}$ long. She folds the paper into a dragon that is  $2\dfrac12\text{ cm}$ long.

**How much longer was the original paper than the dragon?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] centimeters
hints: We need to subtract to find out how much longer the paper was than the dragon.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then look at the fractions first:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{6} &\blueD\\\\
-&\maroonD{2}&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}\\
\hline{}
\end{align}$

We can't subtract the $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$, because we don't have any halves to subtract from.

$\blueD{6}$ can be regrouped so that it also has a fractional part and we can subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{6} &= \blueD{5+  1} \\
\\
&= \blueD{5 +  \dfrac{2}{2}} \\
\\
&= \blueD{5 \dfrac{2}{2}} 
\end{align}$

Now, subtract the fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{5} &\blueD{\dfrac{2}{2}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{2}&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{}&\greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}
\end{align}$

Next, subtract the whole numbers:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{5} &\blueD{\dfrac{2}{2}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{2}&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{3}&\greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}
\end{align}$
The piece of paper was $3\dfrac12$ centimeters longer than the dragon.

items.question_content:
Kayden walks $\dfrac{4}{5}$  kilometer to a friend's house. Then he and his friend walk $1\dfrac3{5}$ kilometers more to go fishing at their favorite pond.

**How far did Kayden walk all together?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] kilometers
hints: We need to add the distance Kayden walked to his friend's house and the distance they walked to the pond to find out how far Kayden walked all together.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then add the fractions first. 

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{} &\blueD{\dfrac{4}{5}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{7}{5}}
\end{align}$

Because $\greenD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ is greater than $1$, it must be converted into a mixed number.

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\dfrac{7}{5}} &= \greenD{\dfrac{5}{5} +  \dfrac{2}{5}} \\
\\
&= \greenD{1  \dfrac{2}{5}} 
\end{align}$

Then, we put the $\greenD{\dfrac{2}{5}}$ under the fractions and the $\greenD{1}$ with the other whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\greenD{1} \\
&\blueD{} &\blueD{\dfrac{4}{5}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{2}{5}}
\end{align}$

Next, add the whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\greenD{1} \\
&\blueD{} &\blueD{\dfrac{4}{5}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD2&\greenD{\dfrac{2}{5}}
\end{align}$
Kayden walked $2\dfrac25$ kilometers all together.

items.question_content:
Frank's dog Dynamite spent $3\dfrac9{12}$ minutes playing in the mud. Later, Frank spent $8\dfrac5{12}$ minutes giving Dynamite a bath.

**How much longer did Dynamite spend getting a bath than playing in the mud?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] minutes
hints: We need to subtract to find out how much longer Dynamite spent getting a bath than playing in the mud.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then look at the fractions first:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\blueD{\dfrac5{12}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}\\
\hline{}
\end{align}$

We can't subtract $\blueD{\dfrac{5}{12}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}$, because $\blueD{\dfrac{5}{12}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}$.

$\blueD{8\dfrac{5}{12}}$ can be regrouped so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}$.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{8\dfrac{5}{12}} &= \blueD{8+  \dfrac{5}{12}} \\
\\
&= \blueD{7 +  1 +  \dfrac{5}{12}} \\
\\
&= \blueD{7 +  \dfrac{12}{12} +  \dfrac{5}{12}} \\
\\
&= \blueD{7 \dfrac{17}{12}} 
\end{align}$

Now, subtract the fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{7} &\blueD{\dfrac{17}{12}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{}&\greenD{\dfrac{8}{12}}
\end{align}$

Next, subtract the whole numbers:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{7} &\blueD{\dfrac{17}{12}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{4}&\greenD{\dfrac{8}{12}}
\end{align}$

Dynamite spent $4\dfrac8{12}$ minutes longer getting a bath than playing in the mud.

We can also write this as $4\dfrac23$ minutes.

items.question_content:
Ava's birthday cake is $11\dfrac{7}{10}\text{ cm}$ tall. Her candles stick up from the cake an additional  $5\dfrac4{10}\text{ cm}$.

**What is the total height of the cake and candles?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] centimeters
hints: We need to add to find the total height of the cake and candles.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then add the fractions first. 

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{11} &\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{5}&\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{11}{10}}
\end{align}$

Because $\greenD{\dfrac{11}{10}}$ is greater than $1$, it must be converted into a mixed number.

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\dfrac{11}{10}} &= \greenD{\dfrac{10}{10} +  \dfrac{1}{10}} \\
\\
&= \greenD{1  \dfrac{1}{10}} 
\end{align}$

Then, we put the $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{10}}$ under the fractions and the $\greenD{1}$ with the other whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\greenD{1} \\
&\blueD{11} &\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{5}&\pink{\dfrac{4}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{1}{10}}
\end{align}$

Next, add the whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\greenD{1} \\
&\blueD{11} &\blueD{\dfrac{7}{10}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{5}&\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{17}&\greenD{\dfrac{1}{10}}
\end{align}$
The total height of the cake and candles is  $17\dfrac1{10}$ centimeters.

items.question_content:
Kate started with a piece of bubblegum that was $\dfrac58\text{ in}$ wide. She later blew a bubble that was $2\dfrac78\text{ in}$ wide.

**How much wider was Kate's bubble than the original piece of bubblegum?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] inches
hints: We need to subtract to find out how much larger the bubble was than the piece of gum.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then subtract the fractions first:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{2} &\blueD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{2}{8}}
\end{align}$

Next, subtract the whole numbers:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{2} &\blueD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{2}&\greenD{\dfrac{2}{8}}
\end{align}$

Kate's bubble was $2\dfrac28$ inches larger than the original piece of gum.

We can also write this as $2\dfrac14$ inches.

items.question_content:
Archer made a tunnel for his hamster out of two cardboard tubes. One tube was $8\dfrac{5}{8}\text{ in}$ long. The other tube was $7\dfrac6{8}\text{ in}$ long.

**What is the total length of the tunnel?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] inches
hints: We need to add to find the length of both tubes together.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then add the fractions first. 

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\blueD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{7}&\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{11}{8}}
\end{align}$

Because $\greenD{\dfrac{11}{8}}$ is greater than $1$, it must be converted into a mixed number.

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\dfrac{11}{8}} &= \greenD{\dfrac{8}{8} +  \dfrac{3}{8}} \\
\\
&= \greenD{1  \dfrac{3}{8}} 
\end{align}$

Then, we put the $\greenD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$ under the fractions and the $\greenD{1}$ with the other whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\greenD{1} \\
&\blueD{8} &\blueD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{7}&\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{3}{8}}
\end{align}$
The total length of the tunnel is $16\dfrac38$ inches.

items.question_content:
Kay sees a gorilla handprint at the zoo that is $16\dfrac{5}{10}\text{ cm}$ wide. Her own hand is $5\dfrac1{10}\text{ cm}$ wide.

**How much wider is the gorilla hand than Kay's hand?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] centimeters
hints: We need to subtract to find out how much wider the gorilla hand is than Kay's hand.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then subtract  the fractions first:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{16} &\blueD{\dfrac5{10}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{5}&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{}&\greenD{\dfrac{4}{10}}
\end{align}$

Next, subtract the whole numbers:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{16} &\blueD{\dfrac{5}{10}}\\\\
-&\maroonD{5}&\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD{11}&\greenD{\dfrac{4}{10}}
\end{align}$

The gorilla's hand is $11\dfrac4{10}$ cm wider than Kay's hand.

We can also write this as $11\dfrac25$.

items.question_content:
The Bing family spent $1\dfrac{1}{4}$ hours driving to the beach. Then they spent $3\dfrac2{4}$ hours playing in the sand and water.

**How long did the Bing family spend all together driving to and playing at the beach ?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] hours
hints: We need to add to find the total amount of time the Bing family spent driving to and playing at the beach.

We can line up the mixed numbers by their whole number parts and fractional parts, and then add the fractions first. 

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{1} &\blueD{\dfrac{1}{4}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{3}{4}}
\end{align}$

Next, add the whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{1} &\blueD{\dfrac{1}{4}}\\\\
+&\maroonD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD4&\greenD{\dfrac{3}{4}}

The Bing family spent a total of $4\dfrac34$ hours driving to and playing at the beach.

items.question_content:
You are planning a movie marathon.  Each movie lasts $\dfrac32$ hours.  You want to watch $4$ movies nonstop. 

**How many hours will your movie marathon last?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]]  hours
hints:
Each movie lasts $\maroonD{\dfrac32}$ hours.


[[☃ image 1]]

You want to watch $\blueD{4}$ movies nonstop.


[[☃ image 1]]

You can multiply $\blueD{4 \times}\maroonD{\dfrac32}$ to figure out how long your movie marathon will last.

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{4 \times} \maroonD{\dfrac32}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{4 \times}\maroonD3} {\maroonD2}\\\\
&={\dfrac{12}{2}}\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$
Your movie marathon will last $6$ hours.

items.question_content:
Last week, Becky went to dance class $4$ times. Each class was $\dfrac32$ hours.

This week, Becky went to dance class $5$ times. Each class was $1$ hour.

**Which week did Becky spend more total time in dance class?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Last week

Last week, Becky went to class $4$ times. Each class was $\dfrac32$ an hour long.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{4 \times \dfrac32}$ to figure out the  time Becky spent in dance class last week: 

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{4 \times \dfrac32}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{4 \times}\blueD 3} {\blueD2}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{12}{2}}\\\\
&=\blueD{6}
\end{align}$

Becky went to class for $\blueD{6}$ hours last week.

###This week

This week, Becky went to class $5$ times. Each class was $1$ an hour long.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{5 \times 1}$ to figure out the  time Becky spent in dance class this week: 

$\maroonD5\times\maroonD1=\maroonD5$

Becky went to class for $\maroonD{5}$ hours this week.

Last week |  | This week
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\blueD{6}$ hours | $>$ | $\maroonD{5}$ hours
Becky spent more total time in dance class last week.

choices.content: She spent the same amount of time in dance class each week.

items.question_content:
Raul bought $6$ tangerines and ate $\dfrac23$ of them.

Omar bought $8$ tangerines and ate $\dfrac14$ of them.

**Who ate more tangerines?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Eating a fraction of the tangerines is the same as eating that fraction times the number of tangerines each person had.

###Raul

Raul ate $\blueD{\dfrac23 \text{ of }6}$ tangerines.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{\dfrac23\times6}$ to figure out the  amount of tangerines Raul ate: 

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\dfrac23\text{ of }6} &=~ \blueD{\dfrac23 \times 6}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{2\times6}{3}}\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac{12}{3}}\\\\
&= \blueD4
\end{align}$

Raul ate $\blueD4$ tangerines.

###Omar 

Omar ate $\maroonD{\dfrac14 \text{ of }8}$ tangerines.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{\dfrac14\times8}$ to figure out the  amount of tangerines Omar ate:

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{\dfrac14\text{ of }8} ~&=~ \maroonD{\dfrac14 \times 8}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{1\times8}{4}}\\\\
&= \maroonD{\dfrac{8}{4}}\\\\
&= \maroonD2
\end{align}$

Omar ate $\maroonD2$ tangerines.

Liz and Erin are different ages but eat from the same size of bowl.

Liz ate $4 $ servings of cereal. Each serving was $\dfrac13 $ of a bowl.

Erin ate $3$ servings of cereal. Each serving was $\dfrac23 $ of a bowl.

**Who ate more bowls of cereal?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Liz

Serving $\maroonD1$ | Serving $\maroonD2$  | Serving $\maroonD3$ | Serving $\maroonD4$ 
:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: 
![Four equal-sized rectangles, each divided into three equal parts with one part shaded pink. Below the rectangles, each rectangle is labeled one-third. Above the rectangles, the rectangles are numbered Serving 1 to Serving 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fd2b3e4cf7cbec4529e84371df31b31a729b41)

We can multiply $\maroonD{4 \times \dfrac13}$ to figure out how many bowls of cereal Liz ate:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\maroonD{4 \times \dfrac13}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{4 \times}\maroonD1} {\maroonD3}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}
\end{align}$

Liz ate $\maroonD{\dfrac43}$ bowls of cereal.

###Erin

Serving $\blueD1$ | Serving $\blueD2$  | Serving $\blueD3$
:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: |:-: 
![Three equal-sized rectangles, each divided into three equal parts with two parts shaded blue. Below the rectangles, each rectangle is labeled two-thirds. Above the rectangles, each rectangle is numbered Serving 1 to Serving 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c0d91f39fea9690a19ba8bc1d408680944d75eea) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c0d91f39fea9690a19ba8bc1d408680944d75eea) | ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c0d91f39fea9690a19ba8bc1d408680944d75eea)

We can multiply $\blueD{3 \times \dfrac23}$ to figure out how many bowls of cereal Erin ate:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{3 \times \dfrac23}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{3 \times}\blueD2} {\blueD3}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{6}{3}}
\end{align}$

Erin ate $\blueD{\dfrac63}$ bowls of cereal.

Liz |  | Erin
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}$ bowls | $<$ | $\blueD{\dfrac{6}{3}}$ bowls
Erin ate more bowls of cereal.

choices.content: They ate the same amount of cereal.

Tess ran $\dfrac43 $ kilometers each day for $2$ days.

Cam ran $\dfrac23 $ of a kilometer each day for $5$ days.

**Who ran more kilometers in total?**

Tess ran $\dfrac43 $ kilometers each day for $2$ days.

$\begin{align}
&\quad\maroonD{2 \times \dfrac43}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{2 \times}\maroonD4} {\maroonD3}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{3}}
\end{align}$

Tess ran $\maroonD{\dfrac83}$ kilometers.

###Cam

Cam ran $\dfrac23 $ of a kilometer each day for $5$ days.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{5 \times \dfrac23}$ to figure out the total distance Cam ran:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{5 \times \dfrac23}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{5 \times}\blueD2} {\blueD3}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{10}{3}}
\end{align}$

Cam ran $\blueD{\dfrac{10}3}$ kilometers.

Tess |  | Cam
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{3}} \text{ km}$ | $<$ | $\blueD{\dfrac{10}{3}} \text{ km}$
Cam ran more kilometers in total.

choices.content: They ran the same number of total kilometers.

items.question_content:
The pepper plant has $\dfrac{2}{3}$ as many fruits on it as the tomato plant has. The tomato plant has $9$ fruits on it. 

**How many fruits does the pepper plant have on it?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] fruits
hints:
A tomato plant has $9$ fruits on it.

![9 circles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/25f5c8fbc09be9480b3c740ba974dae49fdfe9be)

The pepper plant has $\dfrac23$ as many fruits as the tomato plant.

Let's break the $9$ tomatoes into $3$ equal groups.

![The same 9 circles in 3 groups of 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3c6172f2db0c563ee435bfa4cab1f0b0cfb48b21)

The pepper plant has $2$ of the $3$ equal groups.

![6 circles in 2 groups of 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a7b84b16f38612f53c8a4b0a69b9d739ac0e58c5)

There are $6$ peppers.

Since the pepper plant has $\dfrac{2}{3}$ as many fruits, we can also multiply $9 \times \dfrac{2}{3}$ to find how many fruits are on each plant.

$\begin{align}
&9 \times \dfrac{2}{3}\\\\
=&\dfrac{9 \times 2} {3}\\\\
=&\dfrac{18}{3}\\\\
=&\dfrac61\\\\
=&{6}
\end{align}$
The pepper plant has $6$ fruits on it.

There are $2$ chew toys.

Since Annie's dog has $\dfrac{1}{4}$ as many chew toys, we can also multiply $8 \times \dfrac{1}{4}$ to find how many chew toys she has.

$\begin{align}

=&\dfrac{8 \times 1} {4}\\\\
=&\dfrac{8}{4}\\\\
=&{2}
\end{align}$
Annie's dog has $2$ chew toys.

items.question_content:
Mark and Damien were in a hot dog eating contest. Mark ate $12$ hot dogs. Damien ate $\dfrac46$ as many hot dogs as Mark.

**How many hot dogs did Damien eat?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] hot dogs
hints:
Mark ate $12$ hot dogs.

=&\dfrac{12 \times 4} {6}\\\\
=&\dfrac{48}{6}\\\\
=&{8}
\end{align}$
Damien ate $8$ hot dogs.

khan_exercises.title: Divide whole numbers by fractions
khan_exercises.description: Make sense of dividing whole numbers by non-unit fractions with a visual model.

items.question_content:
Use the number line to answer the following $2$ questions.

[[☃ image 1]]

1. **How many groups of $\dfrac{5}{7}$ are in $1$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] groups

2. **Evaluate.**

$8\div \dfrac{5}{7}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can count the number of groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ from the diagram. Since $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ is less than $1$, we will expect to find more than $1$ group.

The expression $\purpleD{8}\div \blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ can mean, "How many groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ are in $\purpleD{8}$?" There will be $\purpleD{8}$ times as many groups in $\purpleD{8}$ as there were in $1$.

###Counting the groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $1$

Since there are $7$ sevenths per $1$ unit, there are between $1$ and $2$ full groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $1$. 

It takes $5$ parts for each full group, and there are $2$ parts left over, for a total of $7$ out of $5$ parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are exactly $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $1$. 

We call $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ the **reciprocal** of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$.

###Finding the groups of $\dfrac{5}{7}$ in $\purpleD{8}$

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $1$. 

So there are $\purpleD{8}\times \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $\purpleD{8}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{8}\div \blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}} &= \purpleD{8} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac{8}{1}} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}\\\\
&= \goldE{\dfrac{56}{5}}
\end{align}$

###Summary

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ groups of ${\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $1$. 

$8\div {\dfrac{5}{7}} = \goldE{\dfrac{56}{5}}$

$4$ unit squares each have $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of their area shaded.

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] square units
hints:
There are $4$ unit squares. $\blueE{\dfrac45}$ of each square is shaded.

items.question_content:
Priya is making a table with expressions that are equal to $6\times\dfrac25$.

**Help Priya complete the table.**

 | |
:- | :- |
$6\times\dfrac25$ | $= $ | $12\times\dfrac15$  
$6\times\dfrac25$| $ = $ | $4\times\dfrac35$    
$6\times\dfrac25$ | $=$ | $2\times{}$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
Priya was able to find several equivalent expressions to $6\times\dfrac25$. 

Let's see how she did it!

##Why does $6\times\dfrac{2}{5}= 12 \times \dfrac{1}{5}$ ?

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=} 6\times\blueD{\dfrac25}\\\\
&= 6 \times \left({\blueD2\times \blueD{\dfrac{1}{5}}}\right)\\\\
&= (6 \times\blueD2) {\times \blueD{\dfrac{1}{5}}}\\\\
&= 12 \times \dfrac{1}{5}
\end{align}$

We can also visualize the expressions:


[[☃ image 1]]

The expressions $6\times\dfrac{2}{5}$ and $12 \times \dfrac{1}{5}$ are on the same point on the number line, so they are equivalent.

##Why does $6\times\dfrac{2}{5}= 4 \times \dfrac{3}{5}$ ?

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=} 6\times{\dfrac25}\\\\
&= \maroonD{12} \times \dfrac{1}{5}\\\\
&=(\maroonD4\times\maroonD3) \times \dfrac15\\\\
&=\maroonD4 \times \left( \maroonD3\times\dfrac15 \right)\\\\
&=\maroonD4 \times \dfrac{\maroonD3}5
\end{align}$

We can also visualize the expressions:



[[☃ image 1]]

The expressions $6\times\dfrac{2}{5}$ and $4 \times \dfrac{3}{5}$ are on the same point on the number line, so they are equivalent.

##What completes $6\times\dfrac{2}{5}= 2 \times \square$ ?

Now, we need to find the missing information for the equivalent expression $2\times\square{}$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=} \purpleE6\times{\dfrac25}\\\\
&=(\purpleE2\times\purpleE3) \times {\dfrac{2}{5}}\\\\
&=\purpleE2 \times \left(\purpleE3\times{\dfrac{2}{5}}\right)\\\\
&= 2 \times \dfrac{6}{5}
\end{align}$

We can also visualize the expressions:

[[☃ image 1]]

The expressions $6\times\dfrac{2}{5}$ and $2 \times \dfrac{6}{5}$ are on the same point on the number line, so they are equivalent.

Here is the complete table:

 | |
:- | :- |
$6\times\dfrac25$ | $= $ | $12\times\dfrac15$  
$6\times\dfrac25$| $ = $ | $4\times\dfrac35$    
$6\times\dfrac25$ | $=$ | $2\times\purpleE{\dfrac65}$
